questions.questionText: The most common cause of right-sided heart failure is:
questions.explanation: Left heart failure increases pulmonary venous pressure, leading to right heart failure.

questions.questionText: Stable angina pectoris is typically caused by:
questions.optionA: Atherosclerotic plaque obstructing >70% of a coronary artery
questions.optionD: Plaque rupture with complete thrombus occlusion
questions.explanation: Fixed strictures cannot increase blood flow during exertion, causing reversible ischemia.

questions.questionText: The most specific and sensitive serum biomarker for acute myocardial infarction is:
questions.explanation: Troponins are highly sensitive and specific for myocardial damage.

questions.questionText: What is the most common complication and cause of death in the first few hours following an acute myocardial infarction?
questions.explanation: Electrical instability surrounding the ischemic area leads to fatal arrhythmias.

questions.questionText: Fibrinous pericarditis (a 'bread and butter' appearance) is a classic finding in:
questions.explanation: Fibrin-rich exudate causes a shaggy surface on the pericardium.

questions.questionText: Rheumatic fever is a consequence of pharyngeal infection by which organism?
questions.explanation: Antibodies against M-protein cross-react with cardiac tissue (molecular mimicry).

questions.questionText: The classic histological finding in acute rheumatic carditis is the:
questions.explanation: Aschoff bodies are granulomatous lesions containing Anitschkow cells (caterpillar cells).

questions.questionText: The most common cause of infective endocarditis in intravenous drug abusers is:
questions.optionA: Viridans streptococci involving the mitral valve
questions.optionB: Candida albicans involving the pulmonary valve
questions.optionC: Staphylococcus aureus involving the tricuspid valve
questions.explanation: S. aureus aggressively attacks the first valve it reaches from systemic venous entry.

questions.questionText: Hypertrophic cardiomyopathy (HCM) is most often caused by mutations in genes encoding:
questions.explanation: HCM is an autosomal dominant disorder of sarcomere proteins.

questions.questionText: A 'tear' in the aortic intima with blood tracking into the media constitutes:
questions.explanation: This dissection creates a false lumen, often presenting with sudden 'tearing' chest pain.

questions.questionText: In Cardiovascular Pathology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Pathology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Pathology references for Cardiovascular Pathology. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Cardiovascular Pathology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical Cardiovascular Pathology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Pathology. Chapter: Cardiovascular Pathology.
